votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the logic of the Coq specification, returning the appropriate list comparison based on the total character counts.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by checking the total character counts and returning whether the result matches the expected list based on the condition.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, returning True if and only if the result matches the list with fewer total characters (or the first list in case of a tie).

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. string_length uses Python's len() which matches the Coq recursive character count. total_chars sums the lengths of all strings in the list, matching the Coq definition. total_match_spec checks if result equals lst1 when c1 <= c2, and equals lst2 when c1 > c2. The Coq Prop uses conjunction of two implications, but for any concrete inputs, exactly one of c1 <= c2 or c1 > c2 holds, so the Python if-else correctly captures the same behavior: when c1 <= c2, the first implication requires result = lst1 (and the second is vacuously true); when c1 > c2, the second implication requires result = lst2 (and the first is vacuously true). The Python returns true/false matching whether the Coq Prop would be satisfied.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec. string_length returns the length of a string, total_chars sums lengths over a list, and total_match_spec checks if result equals lst1 when c1 <= c2, otherwise checks if result equals lst2. The Coq Prop uses conjunction of two implications which together specify the same behavior: when c1 <= c2, result must be lst1; when c1 > c2, result must be lst2. The Python boolean function returns true exactly when the result satisfies this specification for the given concrete inputs.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec. string_length uses len() which matches the Coq recursive character count. total_chars sums lengths identically. total_match_spec checks the same condition: when c1 <= c2, result must equal lst1; when c1 > c2, result must equal lst2. The Coq Prop uses conjunction of implications which is equivalent to the Python if-else logic for concrete inputs - exactly one branch applies for any concrete c1, c2 values.
Python mirrors Coq: it sums string lengths and checks if result equals lst1 when c1<=c2, else equals lst2. This matches the Coq implications for all concrete list-of-strings inputs.

Python computes string length and total characters the same way as Coq for strings, and it checks the same condition: return/result equals lst1 if c1<=c2, else equals lst2. This matches the Coq Prop’s truth conditions for all concrete inputs of lists of strings.
string_length uses length, total_chars sums lengths, and total_match_spec returns True exactly when result equals lst1 if c1<=c2 else lst2, matching the Coq implications on all typical string inputs.
